title: The Soil in Which the Fruit of the Spirit Grows
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 9-15-24 -AM- The Soil in which the Fruit of the Spirit Grows - Galatians 5 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: The Fruit Comes from the Soil
subpoints: The fruit of the Spirit is singular — all nine characteristics present in every believer
The goal is not to cultivate the fruit directly but the soil in which it grows
The soil is walking in the Spirit

outline.title: Defining the Key Terms
subpoints: The Spirit: the Holy Spirit of God, mentioned seven times in the passage
The flesh: our sinful nature still rebelling against God
Walking in the Spirit: continually living according to what the Spirit reveals in his Word
Not under the law: freed from the curse and bondage of the ceremonial law through Christ
Led by the Spirit: following what the Spirit says in Scripture, not a subjective inner experience

outline.title: The Struggle Between the Spirit and the Flesh
subpoints: Walking in the Spirit is a guarantee against fulfilling the lusts of the flesh
Paul uses a double negative: the believer will really not gratify the flesh
The struggle intensifies as we grow in Christ — this is the normal Christian experience
The works of the flesh are examples of what the Spirit empowers us to resist

outline.title: Walking in the Spirit in Practice
subpoints: Walking in the Spirit avoids legalism and license
In the Galatian context it looks like loving and serving one another
The struggle leads us to Christ, who is our only hope

summary: In the opening sermon of the Fruit of the Spirit series, Pastor Tito Lyro examines Galatians 5:16-18 to establish the essential context for understanding the fruit of the Spirit. He argues that the nine characteristics listed in Galatians 5:22-23 are not behaviors to be pursued directly, but byproducts of a life cultivated in the right soil — namely, walking in the Spirit. He carefully defines five key terms: the Spirit (the Holy Spirit of God), the flesh (the sinful nature still in rebellion), walking in the Spirit (continually living according to what the Spirit reveals in Scripture), not under the law (freed from the curse and bondage of the ceremonial law in Christ), and led by the Spirit (following Scripture rather than subjective experience). He explains that the fruit of the Spirit is singular — every Christian exhibits all nine characteristics simultaneously, though at varying intensities. He further explains that walking in the Spirit provides a certain guarantee against fulfilling the lusts of the flesh, but also that the struggle between Spirit and flesh intensifies as Christians grow — a mark of normal sanctification. The sermon concludes by showing that in the Galatian context, walking in the Spirit is expressed practically by using Christian freedom to love and serve one another.
transcript:
## Introduction: Standing on the Word

If you're able to remain standing for a moment longer and for the reader of God's Word this morning, we're turning to Galatians chapter 5. We read the entire chapter. We started a new series this morning on verses 16 through 25 of Galatians chapter 5.

*[Scripture reading: Galatians 5:1-26]*

This is the word of our Lord. Let us pray together. Father, thank you for your word. We pray to speak to us. We pray, Father, that these words will be helpful to your people. In Jesus' name, Amen. Please be seated.

## The Series and Its Purpose

We're starting a 12-part series on Galatians 5:16 through 25, which people would call a series on the fruit of the Spirit. But we often consider the fruit of the Spirit in Galatians 5:22 through 23 without considering the soil in which the fruit grows.

In the context of Galatians, love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, goodness, faithfulness, gentleness, and self-control are not behaviors that are pursued directly. Instead, they result from something else. The word *fruit* implies that. The fruit comes from something else. We don't cultivate the fruit. We cultivate the tree or the bush that's going to bring the fruit. And the fruit is a byproduct of the cultivation of the bush or of the tree.

Though we will consider each fruit eventually separately — love, joy, and so on, all nine different fruits that come from the Spirit — the Holy Spirit is not presenting them here as separate results in the life of the Christian. Notice that Paul says the *fruit* — singular. The fruit of the Spirit is a singular word, and then he lists these nine behaviors or characteristics. The implication is that every Christian will manifest all of these behaviors at all times, albeit at different levels. There is not a time when a Christian doesn't have the fruit of joy but has the fruit of love. All these things are together at the same time as one fruit of the Spirit, manifested in different intensities in the lives of the Christian.

This morning I want us to consider the soil in which the fruit of the Spirit grows. The fruit is the result. What is the soil that springs up this kind of fruit? We'll see that the fruit of the Spirit grows in the rich soil of walking in the Spirit.

## Five Key Terms

Let's read again verses 16 through 18: *I say then, walk in the Spirit and you shall not fulfill the lusts of the flesh. For the flesh lusts against the Spirit and the Spirit against the flesh, and these are contrary to one another, so that you do not do the things that you wish. But if you are led by the Spirit, you are not under the law.*

As we look at these three verses, I want to start by defining some words. There are five terms we need to define: Spirit, flesh, walking in the Spirit, not under the law, and led by the Spirit.

**The Spirit.** This is the Holy Spirit of God. The word *spirit* is mentioned seven times in this short paragraph. The fruit being produced comes from the Holy Spirit of God, and the soil from which the fruit grows is the walking in the Spirit.

**The flesh.** This is what we are by our natural birth — our old nature, our sinful nature. The flesh is the part of us that remains committed to rebelling against God. One of the three great enemies of the Christian — the world, the devil, and the flesh — the self that is still rebelling against God.

**Walking in the Spirit.** The term *to walk* in Paul's writings is a metaphor for living. We could translate this as *continually live in the Spirit* or *continually live by the Spirit*. To walk by the Spirit is to live according to what the Spirit directs. In John 14:26, the Lord Jesus Christ says: *The Helper, the Holy Spirit, whom the Father will send in my name, He will teach you all things and bring to your remembrance all things that I said to you.* Walking by the Spirit is walking according to what the Spirit says in his Word. And a big part of walking in the Spirit is testifying of Christ, because the primary job of the Spirit is to elevate Jesus Christ.

**Not under the law.** In the context of Galatians, the law here is not referring to the moral law — the 10 commandments, love God and love others. That law is never suspended. Rather, Paul refers to the ceremonial law: sacrifices, dietary laws, circumcision. Christ has fulfilled all that law. If we are led by the Holy Spirit, we are no longer under the curse of the law or under the bondage of the ceremonial law.

**Led by the Spirit.** Paul here restates what he said at the beginning of the passage regarding walking in the Spirit. To be led by the Spirit is to be led by what the Spirit says — the Bible. This is not a subjective experience in which we receive a unique message from the Spirit. When we are led by what the Spirit says, we display the fruit of the Spirit.

## The Struggle Between Spirit and Flesh

Now that we understand what these terms mean, we can talk about the struggle that Paul describes between the Spirit and the flesh.

In verse 16, Paul says: *Walk in the Spirit and you shall not fulfill the lusts of the flesh.* He's not saying *try hard*. The walking and the fulfilling are not two independent actions. Paul is saying that if we walk in the Spirit, we will not fulfill or gratify the lust of the flesh — it is a certainty. He even uses a double negative in the original language: *not not.* You will *really not* fulfill the lusts of the flesh if you are walking in the Spirit.

Why then do we still sin? Because we sin because we want to. We still have a part of us that chooses to let the flesh dictate what we are going to do.

Verse 17: *For the flesh lusts against the Spirit and the Spirit against the flesh.* Paul is speaking about those who have been born again. Those outside of Christ do not struggle this way — they don't have the Spirit of God. The more we strive to follow Christ, the more the flesh is going to struggle with us. There are more internal struggles in the Christian life than in the unbelieving life because the Christian actually resists sin. The struggle is the beautiful part of sanctification — if we are struggling, it means we are resisting. And that is the normal Christian experience.

## Walking in the Spirit in Practice

Our flesh tends to misuse the liberty we have in Christ — sometimes corrupting it into legalism, sometimes into license. But as we continue to walk in the Spirit, we'll not relapse into bondage, nor will we use our liberty in Christ as an excuse for sin.

In the context of Galatians, walking in the Spirit looks like serving one another through love. Martin Luther wrote: *Serving one another in love is performing unimportant works such as the following: teaching the erring, comforting the afflicted, encouraging the weak, helping the neighbor in whatever way we can, bearing with his rude manners and impoliteness, putting up with annoyances.* Walking in the Spirit is just living life for the sake of one another.

So if we want love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, goodness, faithfulness, gentleness, and self-control in our lives, the way to pursue them is by walking in the Spirit — according to what the Spirit has revealed in his Word. This walk is not a one-time effort but a constant effort, as we fight the flesh.

Father, we thank you that you are God who is powerful and who works powerfully in us by your Spirit. Help us to walk in the Spirit that the fruit of the Spirit will manifest itself in us. We pray this in Jesus' name, Amen.
